output.final_deliverable:
## Claim-by-claim fact-check

### C1 [Physics]
**Claim:** “The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second.”

**Verdict:** **PARTIALLY TRUE** (value is correct, but in SI it is *exact*, not merely approximate)

**Supporting evidence (value matches):**
- Wikipedia’s *Speed of light* article states: “**The value 299,792,458 metres per second** is approximately 1 billion kilometres per hour; 700 million miles per hour.” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light)

**Contradicting/qualifying evidence (it’s defined exactly, not approximately):**
- The same Wikipedia page says the value is exact by definition: “**It is exact because, by international agreement, a metre is defined as the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of 1⁄299792458 second**.” (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light)
- NIST SP 330 (SI Units) gives the definition of the metre implying an exact speed of light: “**one meter is the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval with duration of 1/299 792 458 of a second**….” (https://www.nist.gov/pml/special-publication-330/sp-330-section-2)

**Confidence:** **0.95**

**Justification:** The numerical value is correct, but authoritative sources (Wikipedia and NIST SP 330) indicate it is *exact by definition*, making the wording “approximately” misleading. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light, https://www.nist.gov/pml/special-publication-330/sp-330-section-2)

---

### C2 [Art History]
**Claim:** “Leonardo da Vinci painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel.”

**Verdict:** **FALSE**

**Contradicting evidence (who actually painted it):**
- Wikipedia’s *Sistine Chapel ceiling* article identifies Michelangelo (not Leonardo) as the painter, stating: “**Prior to Michelangelo** …” in context of the ceiling’s painted elements and commissioning (the page is specifically about the ceiling that Michelangelo painted). (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling)

**Note on evidence quality:** The retrieved evidence clearly points away from Leonardo and toward Michelangelo, but the provided excerpt is truncated and does not include the most explicit sentence (“painted by Michelangelo”). Still, the authoritative page topic and the visible text referencing “Prior to Michelangelo” support the conclusion.

**Confidence:** **0.85**

**Justification:** Authoritative reference (Wikipedia’s dedicated page on the Sistine Chapel ceiling) indicates the ceiling is associated with Michelangelo, contradicting the claim about Leonardo da Vinci. (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling)

---

### C3 [Biology]
**Claim:** “Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.”

**Verdict:** **INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE IN PROVIDED DATA**

**What’s missing:**
- No RAW VISIT CONTENT (visited page text) was provided for octopus anatomy, and the execution trail shows the C3 search node was skipped.

**Confidence:** **0.0**

**Justification:** The dataset contains no visited authoritative source text for octopuses, so the claim cannot be verified or falsified from the evidence provided.

---

### C4 [Geography]
**Claim:** “Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and India.”

**Verdict:** **INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE IN PROVIDED DATA** (but strong search-memory indications it is Nepal–China/Tibet, not Nepal–India)

**Evidence present (from retrieved search memories, not from visited-page text):**
- The retrieved memory includes a snippet: “**Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and the Tibet Autonomous Region of China**….” (snippet recorded in memory; one of the results was https://www.nepalhikingteam.com/where-is-mount-everest)
- Another result listed in memory: Wikipedia’s Mount Everest page (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest) and Britannica (https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest), both of which are typically authoritative for location—but neither was visited in the provided run, so we lack verbatim on-page quotes.

**What’s missing:**
- No RAW VISIT CONTENT for Wikipedia/Britannica/NepalHikingTeam pages about Everest was included, and the visit node for C4 was skipped.

**Confidence:** **0.2**

**Justification:** The only evidence available is search-memory snippets indicating Nepal–Tibet (China), but without visited authoritative-source text in the provided data, the claim cannot be conclusively verified/falsified per the mandate’s standards.

---

## VERDICT REPORT

### Verdict table

| Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence (verbatim quotes) | Source URL |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| C1 | PARTIALLY TRUE | 0.95 | “It is exact because… ‘a metre is defined as… 1⁄299792458 second’.”; “The value 299,792,458 metres per second…”; NIST: “one meter is the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval with duration of 1/299 792 458 of a second” | https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light ; https://www.nist.gov/pml/special-publication-330/sp-330-section-2 |
| C2 | FALSE | 0.85 | Wikipedia ceiling page references Michelangelo: “Prior to Michelangelo …” (showing Michelangelo—not Leonardo—is the relevant artist for the ceiling) | https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling |
| C3 | INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE IN PROVIDED DATA | 0.0 | No visited authoritative source text was provided for this claim. | N/A |
| C4 | INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE IN PROVIDED DATA | 0.2 | Memory snippet: “Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and the Tibet Autonomous Region of China…” (not a visited-page quote in this run) | (snippet referenced) https://www.nepalhikingteam.com/where-is-mount-everest ; also listed but not visited: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest ; https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest |

### Reliability summary (100+ words)
C1 was straightforward to verify because multiple high-authority sources directly address the exact numerical value and the SI definitional status of the speed of light. Wikipedia explicitly states both the number and the definitional basis (“It is exact because…”), and NIST SP 330 provides the SI metre definition that implies the exact value, enabling a precise assessment that the claim’s number is correct but its “approximately” wording is misleading (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light, https://www.nist.gov/pml/special-publication-330/sp-330-section-2). C2 was also relatively straightforward because an authoritative page dedicated to the Sistine Chapel ceiling points to Michelangelo, contradicting Leonardo da Vinci (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sistine_Chapel_ceiling). By contrast, C3 and C4 required deeper investigation but could not be completed from the provided execution results because the relevant search/visit steps were skipped and no authoritative visited-page text was included for octopus physiology or Mount Everest’s border location. The available search-memory snippets suggest Everest is on the Nepal–Tibet (China) border, but without visited citations this remains under-evidenced in the supplied data (https://www.nepalhikingteam.com/where-is-mount-everest; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Everest; https://www.britannica.com/place/Mount-Everest).
output.action_summary: C1: Partially true (value right, but exact by SI definition). C2: False (ceiling is Michelangelo, not Leonardo). C3 and C4: Not verifiable from provided run because no authoritative pages were visited/quoted for those claims; memory snippets suggest Everest is Nepal–Tibet/China, not Nepal–India.

details.goal_evaluation: The original goal requires (a) evidence for/against each of four claims, (b) visiting at least one authoritative source per claim, (c) a TRUE/PARTIALLY TRUE/FALSE verdict per claim with confidence, and (d) a final verdict report including a table and 100+ word reliability summary with cited URLs. The current merged results only provide partial source discovery for C1 and do not include source extraction/visits, verdicts, confidence scores, or any work on C2–C4. Therefore the objective is not met.
missing_requirements: C1: Actually visit/extract at least one authoritative source page (beyond listing it) and provide a final verdict + confidence + justification for C1 (likely PARTIALLY TRUE due to “approximately” vs defined-exact, but needs explicit write-up).
C2: Perform search and visit at least one authoritative source (e.g., Vatican Museums, Britannica, Wikipedia) and determine verdict/confidence.
C3: Perform search and visit at least one authoritative biology/zoology source (e.g., Smithsonian, Britannica, peer-reviewed/academic) and determine verdict/confidence.

C4: Perform search and visit authoritative geography sources (e.g., CIA World Factbook, Britannica, Wikipedia, Nepal/China border descriptions) and determine verdict/confidence (Nepal–China border, not India).
Produce the final VERDICT REPORT: verdict table (Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence | Source URL) and a 100+ word reliability summary; cite every URL used.

synthesized.summary: Only C1 has any gathered evidence, and it is limited to search results/URLs (with one clearly authoritative NIST SI definition page listed). Nodes for C2–C4 were skipped, so no evidence was collected or evaluated for those claims. For C1, the discovered sources indicate the SI defines the metre via the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second, which makes c exactly 299,792,458 m/s; thus the claim phrased as “approximately” is at best partially true/misleading. However, the workflow has not yet completed the required steps: visiting sources and producing verdicts/confidence for all four claims plus the final verdict report.
key_findings: C1: An authoritative NIST SI Brochure-derived page (NIST SP 330, Section 2) states: 1 m is the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of 1/299,792,458 of a second, implying c is fixed exactly at 299,792,458 m/s (https://www.nist.gov/pml/special-publication-330/sp-330-section-2).

C1: Wikipedia explicitly notes the value is exact by international agreement via the metre definition, while also using “approximately” when converting to other units (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speed_of_light).

C1: NIST CODATA value page for c was discovered but not actually visited/extracted; one search result indicates an ill-formed request and another indicates a frames-based page (https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c= and https://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?c|search_for=universal_in!).
C2–C4: No searches, source visits, or evidence were performed because the nodes were skipped; therefore no verdicts can be supported for those claims based on provided results.
synthesized.goal_evaluation: The original goal requires (a) evidence for/against each of four claims, (b) visiting at least one authoritative source per claim, (c) a TRUE/PARTIALLY TRUE/FALSE verdict per claim with confidence, and (d) a final verdict report including a table and 100+ word reliability summary with cited URLs. The current merged results only provide partial source discovery for C1 and do not include source extraction/visits, verdicts, confidence scores, or any work on C2–C4. Therefore the objective is not met.
missing_requirements: C1: Actually visit/extract at least one authoritative source page (beyond listing it) and provide a final verdict + confidence + justification for C1 (likely PARTIALLY TRUE due to “approximately” vs defined-exact, but needs explicit write-up).
C2: Perform search and visit at least one authoritative source (e.g., Vatican Museums, Britannica, Wikipedia) and determine verdict/confidence.
C3: Perform search and visit at least one authoritative biology/zoology source (e.g., Smithsonian, Britannica, peer-reviewed/academic) and determine verdict/confidence.

C4: Perform search and visit authoritative geography sources (e.g., CIA World Factbook, Britannica, Wikipedia, Nepal/China border descriptions) and determine verdict/confidence (Nepal–China border, not India).
Produce the final VERDICT REPORT: verdict table (Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence | Source URL) and a 100+ word reliability summary; cite every URL used.

action_result.raw_response: {"summary":"Only C1 has any gathered evidence, and it is limited to search results/URLs (with one clearly authoritative NIST SI definition page listed). For C1, the discovered sources indicate the SI defines the metre via the distance light travels in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second, which makes c exactly 299,792,458 m/s; thus the claim phrased as “approximately” is at best partially true/misleading. details.merge_skipped_reason: The original goal requires (a) evidence for/against each of four claims, (b) visiting at least one authoritative source per claim, (c) a TRUE/PARTIALLY TRUE/FALSE verdict per claim with confidence, and (d) a final verdict report including a table and 100+ word reliability summary with cited URLs. The current merged results only provide partial source discovery for C1 and do not include source extraction/visits, verdicts, confidence scores, or any work on C2–C4. Therefore the objective is not met.

graph_visualization.ascii:
*-----.   You are a fact-checker.  For each of the following four claims:
  (a) Search for evidence (both supporting and contradicting).
  (b) Visit at least one authoritative source (Wikipedia is acceptable).
  (c) Determine whether the claim is TRUE, PARTIALLY TRUE, or FALSE.
  (d) Provide a confidence score (0.0–1.0) and a short justification citing the source URL.

Claims to verify:

  C1 [Physics]: "The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately 299,792,458 metres per second."
  C2 [Art History]: "Leonardo da Vinci painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel."
  C3 [Biology]: "Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood."
  C4 [Geography]: "Mount Everest is located on the border between Nepal and India."

After verifying all claims, produce a VERDICT REPORT with:
- A verdict table: Claim ID | Verdict | Confidence | Key Evidence | Source URL
- A 100+ word reliability summary discussing which claims were straightforward to verify and which required deeper investigation.
Cite every URL used.
|\ \ \ \  
* | | | | Search authoritative sources for C1 (speed of light exact value) and collect URLs to visit
 / / / /  
* | | | Search authoritative sources for C2 (who painted Sistine Chapel ceiling) and collect URLs to visit
 / / /  
* | | Search authoritative sources for C3 (octopus hearts and blood color) and collect URLs to visit
 / /  
* | Visit authoritative pages already discovered for C4 (Everest border) to extract exact location statement and refute Nepal-India border claim
 /

After verifying all claims, produce a VERDICT REPORT with:

- A 100+ word reliability summary discussing which claims were straightforward to verify and which required deeper investigation.
Cite every URL used.

reasons: All 4 claims are addressed in the agent output (C1–C4).

Only 1 of the 4 verdicts matches the expected verdicts (C2 = FALSE). C1 was labeled PARTIALLY TRUE (expected TRUE), C3 was left INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE (expected TRUE), and C4 was left INSUFFICIENT EVIDENCE (expected FALSE).
Confidence scores were provided for each claim (C1 0.95, C2 0.85, C3 0.0, C4 0.2).
A verdict table is present in the output.
A reliability summary of 100+ words is included and cites URLs, though some claims (C3, C4) lacked visited-page quotes and thus were under-evidenced.
Source URLs are cited for several claims (C1, C2, C4), but C3 lacked any visited authoritative-source text.

details.summary_quality: The reliability summary is adequate: it exceeds 100 words, explains which claims were easy to verify (C1, C2) and which lacked evidence (C3, C4), and cites relevant URLs. However, it leans on 'skipped' or 'memory' snippets for C3 and C4 rather than providing visited-source quotes, which weakens the evidentiary support. The summary correctly identifies strengths and limitations but would be stronger if all four claims had explicit visited authoritative citations.
